full_response:
Step 1 :  Proper time for a “static’’ observer in Schwarzschild spacetime  
For an observer whose spatial Schwarzschild coordinates are fixed (dr = dθ = dφ = 0) the metric  

ds² = – (1 – 2GM/rc²) c² dt² + …  

reduces to  

ds² = – (1 – 2GM/rc²) c² dt² .  

Proper time dτ is defined through ds² = –c² dτ², hence  

dτ = dt √(1 – 2GM/rc²).                (1)

Therefore the tick-rate of the surface clock relative to a clock at r → ∞ (for which dt = dτ∞) is  

dτsurface / dτ∞ = √(1 – 2GM/rc²).                (2)

Step 2 :  Numerical evaluation for the Sun  

G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ m³ kg⁻¹ s⁻²  
c = 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹  
M⊙ = 2 × 10³⁰ kg  
r⊙ = 7 × 10⁸ m  

Compute the dimensionless parameter ε ≡ 2GM/(rc²):

ε = 2(6.674 × 10⁻¹¹)(2 × 10³⁰)
          ────────────────────────
        (7 × 10⁸)(3.00 × 10⁸)²

   = 2.669 × 10²⁰ / 6.30 × 10²⁵  
   ≈ 4.24 × 10⁻⁶.

Step 3 :  Clock-rate ratio  

dτsurface / dτ∞ = √(1 – ε)  
                 ≈ 1 – ε/2            (for ε ≪ 1)  
                 ≈ 1 – 2.12 × 10⁻⁶  
                 ≈ 0.999 997 9.

Step 4 :  Interpretation  

• The surface clock runs slower by about 2.1 µs for every second of a far-away clock.  
• Equivalently it falls behind by ≈ 0.18 s each day.

\boxed{\,\dfrac{d\tau_{\text{surface}}}{d\tau_{\infty}} = \sqrt{1-\dfrac{2GM}{r c^{2}}}\Bigg|_{\text{Sun}} \;\approx\; 0.999\,998 \;=\; 1-2.1\times10^{-6}\,}

Confidence: 93%
